verses.text: And when Saul's son heard that Abner was dead in Hebron, his hands were feeble, and all the Israelites were troubled. Guidance: The news of Abner's death reaches Mahanaim. The Hebrew literally says 'his hands grew weak' — the standard idiom for the loss of political and military capacity. Ish-bosheth had been entirely propped up by Abner's leadership; with Abner dead, the king has no commander, no diplomat, no military genius behind him. He becomes what he had always been potentially: a name on a throne with nothing under it. 'All the Israelites were troubled' — the northern coalition realizes their negotiator is gone and the deal he was brokering with David is uncertain. The chapter opens with collapse. The Saulide kingdom, deprived of Abner, is days away from political extinction. The chapter is going to compress those days into one violent afternoon.

And Saul's son had two men that were captains of bands: the name of the one was Baanah, and the name of the other Rechab, the sons of Rimmon a Beerothite, of the children of Benjamin: (for Beeroth also was reckoned to Benjamin: And the Beerothites fled to Gittaim, and were sojourners there until this day.) Guidance: Introduction of two figures who will end the chapter: Baanah and Rechab, sons of Rimmon the Beerothite. They are Israel-side army officers — 'captains of bands,' command-level military officials in Ish-bosheth's force. The parenthetical detail is interesting. Beeroth was originally a Hivite town near Gibeon, conquered and absorbed into Benjamin (Josh 9:17, 18:25). At some point — probably during Saul's reign — the original Beerothite population fled to Gittaim (a town in Philistine territory) and remained there as exiles. The note is included by the narrator probably to suggest that Rechab and Baanah have a grievance against the Saulide house — Saul, in his pursuit of bloody zeal for ethnic purification (2 Sam 21:1-2 will tell us Saul attacked the Gibeonites, the same Hivite alliance), may have caused the displacement of the Beerothites. If so, the chapter's assassins are not loyal Saulide officers; they are men who carry an old grudge against Saul's family, working from within the regime, waiting for an opportunity. The chapter is showing us how thoroughly compromised Ish-bosheth's inner circle had become. He could not see who his own captains really were.

And Saul's son had two men that were captains of bands: the name of the one was Baanah, and the name of the other Rechab, the sons of Rimmon a Beerothite, of the children of Benjamin: (for Beeroth also was reckoned to Benjamin: And the Beerothites fled to Gittaim, and were sojourners there until this day.) And Jonathan, Saul's son, had a son that was lame of his feet. He was five years old when the tidings came of Saul and Jonathan out of Jezreel, and his nurse took him up, and fled: and it came to pass, as she made haste to flee, that he fell, and became lame. And his name was Mephibosheth. Guidance: A parenthetical introduction of Jonathan's son, Mephibosheth. The narrator inserts him here because he is the only other surviving Saulide who could plausibly inherit the throne after Ish-bosheth, and the chapter is about to show why Mephibosheth never will. He is five years old when the news of Gilboa reaches Jezreel; his nurse picks him up to flee (probably south to safer territory, anticipating Philistine occupation); 'as she made haste to flee, he fell, and became lame.' The detail is precise — she dropped him in the panic; the fall produced permanent disability. In the political world of the period, a physically disabled prince could not be king (ancient Near-Eastern kingship required physical wholeness; even Jacob limping disqualified him from a kind of role). Mephibosheth, by accident of a single fall during evacuation, is removed from succession contention. The narrator is doing two things at once: explaining why Ish-bosheth was the only Saulide heir worth assassinating, and planting the seed of the man David will later recover and seat at his own table in chapter 9. The name 'Mephibosheth' (like Ish-bosheth) probably masks a Baal-name in the original — Merib-baal in 1 Chronicles. The boy will reappear in chapter 9 as one of the most touching figures of David's reign. For now, the narrator wants us to know he exists, is lame, and is too young and disabled to be a political threat to David.

And the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, Rechab and Baanah, went, and came about the heat of the day to the house of Ish-bosheth, who lay on a bed at noon. And they came thither into the midst of the house, as though they would have fetched wheat; and they smote him under the fifth rib: and Rechab and Baanah his brother escaped. For when they came into the house, he lay on his bed in his bedchamber, and they smote him, and slew him, and beheaded him, and took his head, and gat them away through the plain all night. Guidance: The assassination is choreographed with chilling precision. 'About the heat of the day' — noon in the Jordan valley, when most people including the king's bodyguard would be napping. Ish-bosheth himself is napping in his bedroom. The two captains enter the palace under pretext — 'as though they would have fetched wheat' — meaning they had a routine errand (probably grain distribution from royal stores was part of their official duties). They reach the king's bedchamber. They stab him 'under the fifth rib' — the same exact phrase the narrator used of Abner killing Asahel (2:23) and Joab killing Abner (3:27). The repetition of the precise wound-location is intentional; the chapter is showing us that the kingdom's transition has been advancing through a series of identical assassinations. Then they decapitate the king — taking the head for proof — and travel by the Arabah (Jordan valley plain) all night, due south to Hebron, presumably about 80 miles. The chapter is showing us how easily Saul's house collapses once Abner is gone; two mid-level captains can walk into the king's bedroom at midday and execute him. The throne had been hollow for months.

And they brought the head of Ish-bosheth unto David to Hebron, and said to the king, Behold the head of Ish-bosheth the son of Saul thine enemy, which sought thy life; and the LORD hath avenged my lord the king this day of Saul, and of his seed. Guidance: The two captains present the head to David. Their pitch is identical in structure to the Amalekite's in chapter 1: 'Behold the head of Ish-bosheth the son of Saul thine enemy, which sought thy life.' They identify the dead man, name him as David's enemy, and add the religious veneer: 'the LORD hath avenged my lord the king this day of Saul, and of his seed.' They credit God for the killing and present themselves as instruments of divine justice. The lie is on multiple levels. (1) Ish-bosheth was not 'seeking David's life' in any active sense; the two kingdoms had been at war but Ish-bosheth himself was a puppet, not a personally aggressive enemy. (2) The LORD did not authorize this killing. (3) The captains are not 'avenging' on David's behalf; they are pursuing their own gain (and possibly the Beerothite grudge from v3). They expect a reward and are mis-reading David as badly as the Amalekite did. The chapter is going to give them the same answer.

And David answered Rechab and Baanah his brother, the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, and said unto them, As the LORD liveth, who hath redeemed my soul out of all adversity, When one told me, saying, Behold, Saul is dead, thinking to have brought good tidings, I took hold of him, and slew him in Ziklag, who thought that I would have given him a reward for his tidings: How much more, when wicked men have slain a righteous person in his own house upon his bed? shall I not therefore now require his blood of your hand, and take you away from the earth? Guidance: David's response is structured as a legal argument with a precedent. (1) Theological frame: 'As the LORD liveth, who hath redeemed my soul out of all adversity.' David's life has been preserved by God, not by political assassinations. (2) The precedent: 'When one told me, saying, Behold, Saul is dead… I took hold of him, and slew him in Ziklag.' David cites his own chapter-1 execution of the Amalekite as binding precedent. He has done this before; he will do it again. The principle is consistent. (3) The aggravation: 'How much more, when wicked men have slain a righteous person in his own house upon his bed?' Ish-bosheth was 'righteous' in the relevant sense — innocent of any personal capital offense, asleep, defenseless. Killing him in his bed is morally worse than killing Saul on the battlefield. The legal aggravation makes the case for execution even stronger than the precedent. (4) The verdict: 'Shall I not therefore now require his blood of your hand, and take you away from the earth?' Capital sentence. The chapter is showing us the same Davidic policy that began chapter 1: no one is rewarded for killing the LORD's anointed (or in this case, the king-in-name from the previous regime). David is establishing as a permanent rule of his throne that political assassinations brought to him as gifts will receive death, not gold. The throne is being founded again on a public, repeated refusal to accept such gains. This is the second time in five chapters that David executes assassins who expected reward. The pattern is now unmistakable. The new dynasty will not be built on the blood of its predecessors' families.

And David commanded his young men, and they slew them, and cut off their hands and their feet, and hanged them up over the pool in Hebron. But they took the head of Ish-bosheth, and buried it in the sepulchre of Abner in Hebron. Guidance: The execution is carried out and made an example. The two assassins are killed; their hands and feet are cut off (the hands that did the killing, the feet that did the running); the mutilated bodies are hanged 'over the pool in Hebron' as a public display. The mutilation matches the crime: the hands that smote, the feet that fled. The display is meant to be permanent enough that everyone in Hebron sees it and the message travels north. David is publicly announcing to the whole country what happens to political assassins. The Saulide house has just lost both its commander (Abner, ch 3) and its king (Ish-bosheth, ch 4); both murders were unauthorized; both are publicly condemned by the new king. The chapter closes with the burial: 'they took the head of Ish-bosheth, and buried it in the sepulchre of Abner in Hebron.' Ish-bosheth and Abner — the puppet king and his kingmaker — share a single tomb in the capital of David's reign. The chapter is gentle here. David could have desecrated Ish-bosheth's remains; he buries them honorably alongside Abner. The Saulide-northern faction has its last royal leader and his commander resting together, with full burial dignity, in the same town David rules from. The new king is treating his predecessor regime's leaders with the honor their offices, however failed, deserved. The wisdom of the gesture is political and moral. Three chapters into 2 Samuel, every faction in Israel has reason to know: David did not order any of the deaths that brought him this far; David honors the offices even of his former rivals; David is consistent. Tomorrow, chapter 5, all Israel will come to Hebron and anoint him king of the whole country. The work of chapters 2-4 — the patient, public, principled work of refusing every shortcut to the throne — is about to pay its dividend. There is a category of leadership that wins by refusing to win in the ways everyone expected. David has just played that whole game and won. The unification is at hand.
